subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral + independent (IMF, World Bank, CRS, Freedom House, RSF, BTI, Coface, UNHCR) + international press; state media EXCLUDED on governance.

Strategically pivotal at the Bab-el-Mandeb chokepoint - hosts US (Camp Lemonnier), French, Chinese, Japanese, and Italian bases - with a stable 50-year dollar currency board, against unsustainable Chinese debt and a closed succession question.

Standout features: rare macro stability (a credible USD currency board, low inflation, a ports/logistics hub) and indispensable geography, against debt the IMF calls unsustainable (>70% to China), port-revenue concentration, Red Sea disruption, water scarcity, and an entrenched authoritarian succession (Guelleh's 6th term, 2026).

economic.notes: A credible currency board and low inflation, against debt-distress and port-revenue concentration.

fiscal_state.notes: The IMF assesses debt in distress (~70% of GDP, >70% to China), reliant on a 2023 moratorium to 2027, with a narrow non-port revenue base. (IMF; Coface 2025)

monetary_independence.notes: A currency board pegs the franc to the USD at 177.721 (held since 1973) - a deliberate surrender of policy for stability. (Central Bank of Djibouti)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: Not a reserve currency, but >100% USD backing of the monetary base makes the board credible. (CBD)

inflation.notes: Low and stable, anchored by the dollar peg - among the calmest in the region. (IMF; World Bank)

banking_stability.notes: Currency-board credibility supports a regional banking-hub role; never devalued in 75 years. (African Business 2025)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: Ports/logistics are the core competence (transshipment for landlocked Ethiopia), but traffic fell ~10% in 2025 on Red Sea/tariff disruption. (World Bank; Coface 2025)

institutional.notes: A functional port-state core with commercial-law credibility, against weak political rights and a crushed press.

rule_of_law.notes: Executive-dominated courts, with some commercial-law credibility for the ports/free-zone sector but weak political-rights protection. (BTI 2026; Freedom House 2025)

statistical_integrity.notes: Limited transparency and lagged/opaque official data, especially on debt terms. (IMF; BTI)

civil_service_capacity.notes: A functional core state and well-run port authority, but limited human capital and rural reach. (World Bank; BTI 2026)

press_freedom.notes: RSF ~166/180 (2025) - no independent domestic outlet, with jamming and internet throttling. (RSF; Freedom House 2025)

political_social.notes: Near-term stability under entrenched rule, with a deferred succession and Issa-Afar asymmetry.

political_stability.notes: Stable near-term under Guelleh, but the 2025 age-limit removal/2026 sixth term defers an eventual high-stakes succession. (Africanews; ConstitutionNet 2025)

civil_liberties.notes: Multiparty in form only, with opposition constrained and critics harassed/detained. (Freedom House 2025; BTI 2026)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Hosts ~30k+ refugees and major transit migration with limited livelihoods, plus reported Oromo deportations. (UNHCR)

social_cohesion.notes: Broad acceptance of the nation-state, but an Issa-Afar power asymmetry and Ethiopia-origin Afar spillover create friction. (BTI 2026)

demographics.notes: Young but with ~26% unemployment (>70% youth) and weak human capital blunting the dividend. (World Bank 2024)

geopolitical.notes: A multi-aligned 'strategic landlord' with open convertibility, against Red Sea spillover.

alliance_reliability.notes: A deliberately multi-aligned 'strategic landlord' (US/France/China/Japan/Italy bases; a 2024 France defense-treaty renewal) - durable but hedging. (Al Jazeera; CRS 2025-26)

conflict_involvement.notes: Not a combatant in regional wars, but exposed to Red Sea/Houthi disruption, a low-level FRUD-Arme insurgency, and Ethiopia-Afar spillover. (BTI; Horn Review 2025)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: An open currency-board/free-zone regime with generally free convertibility and no major sanctions, though debt-distress constrains flows. (CBD; IMF)

physical_practical.notes: World-class Chinese-built ports/rail and relative safety, against extreme aridity and water scarcity.

climate.notes: Extreme heat, chronic drought, near-total food-import dependence, water scarcity, coastal exposure. (ND-GAIN; WB Horn)

healthcare.notes: Capital-concentrated and donor-supported - better than fragile peers but with thin rural coverage. (World Bank; EU)

infrastructure.notes: World-class Chinese-built ports/rail (Addis-Djibouti) and a submarine-cable hub - a genuine strength, though debt-financed. (CRS; World Bank)

crime_safety.notes: Comparatively low violent crime and one of the safer Horn states for the capital/expat zones. (OSAC)

disaster_insurance.notes: A limited domestic market - drought/water risk largely uninsured and donor-backstopped. (inference, low data)

summary: The rare Horn-of-Africa state that combines genuine macro stability - a 50-year-credible dollar currency board, low inflation, a regional banking and ports hub - with acute structural vulnerabilities: a port/logistics economy (~70% of GDP) over-concentrated on Ethiopia and exposed to Red Sea/Houthi disruption (traffic down ~10% in 2025), and debt the IMF calls unsustainable, more than 70% owed to China and only manageable via a moratorium to 2027. Its real 'oil' is geography: rents from US, French, Chinese, Japanese, and Italian bases at Bab-el-Mandeb underwrite a deliberately multi-aligned foreign policy. Domestically it is stable but firmly authoritarian - Guelleh secured a sixth term in April 2026 after a 2025 age-limit removal, the press is among the world's least free, and an eventual succession plus Issa-Afar asymmetry are the key latent risks.
